🤖 Agentic workflow · Sysco

Smart Assignment

An AI agent that autonomously recommends the best delivery day & time slot for a new customer — running every step end-to-end, enforcing hard operational rules in code and scoring options with deterministic math, then having an LLM reason over those verified facts to make the recommend-or-escalate call and pick the slot — handing off to a human (with a triage sub-agent drafting the brief) whenever the best option falls short.

🤖 Agent-orchestrated end to end 🧠 LLM-grounded, verified decisions ✅ Deterministic rules, scoring & fallback 🧪 Running on mock data
The problem

Onboarding a new account, every time, the same way

When a new customer signs on, someone has to decide which delivery route and time window they should join. That decision balances truck capacity, geography, and the customer's preference — and it needs to be consistent, explainable, and fast. The Smart Assignment agent makes that call automatically, and flags the tricky ones for a specialist instead of guessing.

How the agent works

Five steps, executed autonomously by the agent

🤖
A single AI agent performs all five stages below in one end-to-end run — these are phases of the same agent's workflow, not five separate agents. The hard rules and scoring are deterministic code, but the stage-5 recommend-or-escalate decision is an LLM reasoning over those verified facts. On an escalation it consults one sub-agent (the escalation-triage AgentTool) to write the handoff brief; otherwise a person is involved only if the agent decides to escalate. See the Architecture tab for every agent, sub-agent, and LLM call.
1

Intake

Capture the customer's address, order quantity (cases), and preferred slot (day + time).

Validate the intake profile & build it.

2

Geo-Lookup

Geocode the address and find the nearest candidate routes.

Geocode & pick the Top-3 nearest.

3

Constraint Check

Drop any route that fails a hard rule.

Enforce serviceability & capacity.

4

Score & Rank

Rank survivors on weighted business factors.

Score & order the options.

5

Recommend

An LLM reasons over the verified facts to recommend the top slot — or escalate.

Grounded recommend-or-escalate call.

The rules the agent enforces

Hard constraints — non-negotiable, checked in code

Just two objective facts gate feasibility — not judgment calls. The agent removes any route that fails one before ranking; it can never "reason" a customer onto a full truck or outside the serviceable area. (See the Simulator tab for exactly how the surviving routes are scored.)

📍

Geographic serviceability

The customer must fall within the route's serviceable radius.

📦

Route capacity

The truck stays at or below 90% capacity after adding this order.

🕑 The preferred delivery slot — a day of week plus a time window — is a soft preference, not a hard constraint — it never eliminates a route. Instead it feeds the slot match scoring factor, so a route on a different day or time can still win if it's the best overall fit.

The outcome

Three possible agent decisions

Every run ends in one of three states. When the agent judges the best option too weak to auto-assign (the 60% bar is its reference and deterministic fallback), or there's no valid slot at all, it hands the case to a human — with full context attached.

✔ Recommended

A clear winner whose own total score clears the bar — the agent auto-assigns.

⚠ Low score

A slot is proposed, but its own total score falls short — the agent asks a specialist to confirm.

✖ No feasible slot

Every candidate failed a hard rule — the agent routes it to a specialist.

Architecture

The agentic workflow, end to end

The agent is a Google ADK LlmAgent that talks to the user and calls a tool for each of the five steps — it decides when to call which tool and narrates the result. Every distance, constraint check, and score comes back from deterministic code, but the recommend/escalate decision and the route & slot pick are made by an LLM reasoning over those verified facts — constrained to the feasible options, cited, checked in code, with a deterministic fallback. At Geo-Lookup, when an address won't geocode, a grounded resolver LLM picks the closest of the geocoder's own suggested matches for the user to confirm — it never invents an address. On an escalation it consults a dedicated LlmAgent — the escalation-triage AgentTool — to write the handoff brief. The LLM & agent touchpoints below list every model in the loop.

🤖

Agent orchestrator

An ADK LlmAgent drives all five steps via tool calls, conversationally.

🧭

Deterministic tools

Geo, hard constraints, and weighted scoring — plain code the agent calls.

🤝

A triage sub-agent

On escalation, a dedicated LlmAgent (an ADK AgentTool) composes the specialist brief — read-only, it never changes the decision.

🎯

Selects, never invents

The recommend/escalate call, the route & slot pick, and the on-miss address correction are an LLM's — but only from a deterministically enumerated set (feasible routes; the geocoder's own suggestions), with every cited fact verified in code and a deterministic fallback.

LLM & agent touchpoints

Every place a model is in the loop — and its leash

The deterministic pipeline — geocoding, the hard-constraint filter, and the weighted scoring math — always runs and is the auditable floor. On top of it, several layers put an LLM in the decision loop, each gated by a Config.use_* flag. Three are on by default (grounded address resolution, the recommend-vs-escalate route-slot decision, and the escalation-triage sub-agent); the rest are turned on by the shipped .env — so with real backend credentials the recommend/escalate call, the route & slot pick, and the address correction are made by an LLM reasoning over the verified facts, not by a fixed threshold. Here is every one, called out: what kind of model call it is, what it may touch, and the deterministic guardrail that keeps it honest. Each LLM surface resolves its model per-role via Config.for_role, so roles can run different model tiers while the backend stays global.

Agent

Conversational orchestrator

agent.py · root_agent · ADK LlmAgent · role root_agent

The one agent that drives the whole run. It talks to the user across turns and decides when to call which tool — Intake → Geo-Lookup → Constraint Check → Score & Rank → Recommend/Decide — then narrates the grounded result in its own words.

Guardrail: It never computes a distance, constraint, or score itself, and it never overrides the decision — every number and verdict comes back from the tools (tools/slot_recommendation.py). The conversation is LLM-driven; the numbers are not.
SMART_ASSIGNMENT_MODEL_ROOT_AGENTAlways on (conversational path)
LLM call · FunctionTool

Grounded address resolution

address_resolve/ · resolve_address FunctionTool on root_agent · role address_resolve

When an address won't geocode, instead of a dead-end the agent calls this tool: an LLM picks the closest of the geocoder's own real candidate matches — by index — as a confirmable suggestion for the user. It corrects a typo or ambiguity; it never writes a new address.

Guardrail: The valid options are fixed upstream (the geocoder's candidates, Geocoder.suggest); the LLM picks one by index and must cite the candidates' own facts, verified in code (address_resolve/verifier.py). Any failure falls back to the deterministic highest-similarity candidate, and a human confirms the pick before anything acts on it.
SMART_ASSIGNMENT_USE_ADDRESS_RESOLUTIONOn by default
Sub-agent · AgentTool

Escalation-triage sub-agent

triage/ · an LlmAgent via google.adk.tools.AgentTool · role triage

The first real multi-agent split. On an escalation, root_agent consults a dedicated LlmAgent (exposed as an ADK AgentTool, consult-and-return) to compose the specialist handoff brief: root cause, concrete remediation options, and the question to ask.

Guardrail: Read-only — it only reads session state and runs strictly downstream of the deterministic decision, so it never changes the route, score, or decision. A deterministic prose scan (triage/verifier.py) appends a “⚠ Unverified” caveat to any figure not found in the evaluation trace. root_agent keeps the human-in-the-loop pause.
SMART_ASSIGNMENT_USE_ESCALATION_TRIAGEOn by default
LLM call

Grounded recommend-vs-escalate judgment

judgment/ · role judgment

When enabled, an LLM makes the recommend-or-escalate call itself — reasoning over a structured evidence packet of the raw per-candidate facts — instead of the fixed weighted-sum + 60% threshold gate.

Guardrail: Hard constraints still run first and are the only thing that can drop a candidate, so the LLM chooses only among already-feasible routes. Structured citations are verified in code (judgment/verifier.py), with one corrective retry; escalation-side cases resample up to k=3 and require consensus. Any failure falls back to the deterministic weighted pick — never worse than today.
SMART_ASSIGNMENT_USE_GROUNDED_JUDGMENTOff in code · on in .env.example
LLM call

Grounded route-slot decision

routeslot/ · role judgment

When route-slot scoring is on, the decision unit is the (route, slot) pair, and an LLM makes the recommend-vs-escalate call itself over all feasible route-slots — not a fixed threshold. A confident recommend ships on one verified call; an escalate (or low-confidence recommend) is resampled up to k=3 and combined by consensus before it may auto-assign. It picks the winning route-slot by index and returns a structured trade-off explanation (summary, reasons, key trade-off, runner-up, agree/diverge vs. the weighted default).

Guardrail: The LLM chooses only among deterministically feasible, scored route-slots; its pick and every cited figure are verified in code (routeslot/verifier.py), with one corrective retry. On any failure it falls back to the deterministic 55%-threshold decision — the reproducible floor. Whether the LLM decides escalation is itself gated by USE_GROUNDED_ROUTE_SLOT_ESCALATION (on by default). Absorbs the slot-pick pass.
SMART_ASSIGNMENT_USE_ROUTE_SLOT_SCORINGOff in code · on in .env.example
LLM call

Grounded delivery-slot selection

slotpick/ · role slotpick

After a route is chosen, an LLM picks the final delivery window from that route's deterministically enumerated candidate menu — by index only, reasoning over each candidate's facts. It reasons and selects; it never generates a window. (The route-slot decision above folds this pick into its own grounded call when it runs.)

Guardrail: Constrained to the enumerated candidates and verified against the packet (slotpick/verifier.py); it only re-orders that route's slots, never the route, score, or decision. The hand-tuned deterministic blend is demoted to reference + fallback — the auditable floor.
SMART_ASSIGNMENT_USE_GROUNDED_SLOT_SELECTIONOff in code · on in .env.example
LLM narration

Reasoning narration

reasoning.py · LLMReasoner · role reasoning

An optional reasoner that rewrites the deterministic reasoning trace into more fluent prose for callers of the pipeline directly (e.g. scripts/run_local.py). The conversational agent instead narrates the recommendation in its own words.

Guardrail: Narration only — it never changes a number, a route, or the decision. The deterministic trace (DeterministicReasoner) is the fallback and is exactly what generated this page — so these examples are reproducible offline.
SMART_ASSIGNMENT_MODEL_REASONINGDeterministic by default
🛡️ The invariant across all of them. The deterministic pipeline always runs first and is the floor. An LLM here reasons and selects; it never invents an actionable value — it picks from a deterministically enumerated set (by index/id) and cites the facts it used, which are checked in code before anything acts on them. On any failure — parse, failed verification, missing credentials, backend error — the layer falls back to the deterministic result and logs why. So an LLM layer is strictly additive: never worse than the deterministic baseline, only — when it succeeds — better-reasoned and better-explained.
Key mechanics

What makes it trustworthy

🤖 One agent, five steps

A single ADK LlmAgent calls one tool per step — Intake → Geo-Lookup → Constraint Check → Score & Rank → Decide — and pauses to escalate when needed.

🧭 Hard rules & scoring are code, not vibes

The feasibility filter (serviceability, capacity) and the factor scoring are deterministic Python — identical inputs always yield identical facts, and that deterministic result is always the floor and the fallback.

🎯 The decision is an LLM's — but grounded & verified

With the backend configured, an LLM makes the recommend/escalate call and picks the route & slot. It only ever chooses from a deterministically enumerated, feasible set and cites facts that are checked in code — it never free-generates a route, window, or score, and falls back to the deterministic result on any failure.

🙋 Human-in-the-loop on escalation

The agent pauses and waits for a specialist's reply (via ADK's request_input tool) when it finds no feasible slot, or the best option's own total score falls short of the auto-assign bar — after the triage sub-agent has drafted the brief.

Try it yourself

Run the agent on a customer

Enter a mock customer's address (or pick one below) and watch the agent execute each step — including the scoring math for every feasible route — then render its recommendation. Everything runs in your browser; no data leaves the page.

Sample prospects — click one to load their address into the box above, then press Run agent workflow:
How the agent scores & ranks

Every route-slot is scored on its own

The decision unit is the (route, time-slot) pair: each candidate slot on each feasible route is scored separately, so a route's slot availability — not just its capacity and location — decides which route wins. Two factors are route-level (shared by all of a route's slots) and two are slot-level. Each is normalized to 0–1 and combined by weight; deterministic Python, same inputs → same score.

🧭

Geographic clustering · weight 0.35 route-level

How tightly the customer sits within the route's existing cluster of stops. Closer = higher.

score = clamp( 1 − avg_miles_to_stops ÷ 15 , 0, 1 )
🛡️

Capacity buffer · weight 0.25 route-level

How safely under the capacity ceiling the truck stays once this order is added. Flat while comfortably safe (≤ 75% full), decaying to 0 at the 90% ceiling.

🎯

Slot match (day + time) · weight 0.20 slot-level

How much this slot covers the customer's preferred day + time window. The day is a gate; then it's the overlap fraction. Dropped entirely when the prospect states no preference — the score simply renormalizes over the other factors (no arbitrary neutral value).

🪟

Slot availability · weight 0.20 slot-level

How open this slot is — few / low-tier committed stops already in it. Weighted so we avoid crowding the most valued customers.

openness = 1 ÷ ( 1 + Σ harm(incumbent) over stops sharing the window )

Harm per overlapping committed stop, by tier: 5 / Perks 1.0 > 4 0.6 > the prospect > Other 0.1 (unknown 0.4). A window full of Other-tier stops still scores open; one shared by tier-5 / Perks incumbents scores contended.

Best route-slot, then the auto-assign decision

total = ( 0.35·clustering + 0.25·capacity + 0.20·window* + 0.20·availability ) ÷ Σ active weights

*window only when a preference is stated; otherwise it's absent and the remaining weights renormalize. This weighted total ranks the route-slots; the 55% bar is the deterministic reference. With the backend configured, an LLM reasons over all feasible route-slots and makes the recommend-vs-escalate call itself — a confident recommend ships on one verified call, while an escalate (or low-confidence recommend) is resampled and cleared by consensus before it may auto-assign. It picks the winning route-slot by index from the feasible set and cites the facts; every choice is verified in code, and on any failure it falls back to the deterministic 55%-bar decision — the reproducible floor.

Where the numbers come from

Every threshold, and its source

The formulas above use real values from three places. Config defaults are tunable in shared/config.py; per-route numbers (capacity, radius) are mock data standing in for a real routing system; the rest comes from the customer's intake.

⚙️ Config defaults shared/config.py

  • Route capacity ceiling — max_utilization_after_assignment90%
  • Capacity safety margin (safe up to) — capacity_buffer_safety_margin15% pts (→ 75%)
  • Clustering reference (score→0) — cluster_reference_miles15 mi
  • Route-slot weights (geo/cap/win/avail) — rs_weight_*0.35 / 0.25 / 0.20 / 0.20
  • Slot-openness harm (5·Perks/4/Other/unknown) — slot_tier_harm_*1.0 / 0.6 / 0.1 / 0.4
  • Route-slot auto-assign bar — route_slot_score_threshold55%
  • Serviceability hard cap — max_service_distance_miles25 mi
  • Candidates evaluated — top_n_candidate_routesTop-3

🗄️ Route data mocked TMS

  • Vehicle capacity per route — denominator of utilization %700–950 cases
  • Service radius per route — the serviceability limit12–16 mi
  • Committed stops + locations — drive clustering & used capacityvaries
  • Available delivery windows — window overlapvaries

From integrations/route_capacity_client.py.

🧾 Customer intake new customer

  • Address → geocoded point — geocoding — the primary identifierper customer
  • Order quantity — capacity mathcases
  • Preferred slot: day + time (optional) — slot scoring onlysoft
  • Sysco customer number (optional) — unset for prospects; matched if on fileplaceholder

Env vars can override any config default (e.g. SMART_ASSIGNMENT_MAX_UTILIZATION).

All outcomes at a glance

What the agent decided for each mock customer

These cards are generated straight from the agent's output. Each customer lands on a different outcome; expand “Routes the agent evaluated” to audit the full decision.

new prospect — no Sysco number yet

Bayou City Bistro

1200 McKinney St, Houston, TX 77010
90 cases prefers TUE 07:00–10:00 3 routes evaluated
✔ Recommended total score 82%

→ RTE-4100 · Central Houston · TUE · 07:20–10:20 (score 0.82)

Geographic clustering
0.94
Capacity buffer
1.00
Slot match (day + time)
0.89
Slot availability (openness)
0.33
Why the agent chose thisAssign RTE-4100 - Central Houston · TUE · 07:20-10:20.
  • Geographic fit: avg 0.8 mi to existing stops.
  • Capacity headroom: 290 cases of headroom left, putting the truck at about 68% full after this order (comfortably safe up to 75%).
  • Preferred-window match: covers 160 of the 180 preferred minutes on Tuesday.
  • Slot openness: tier-weighted contention 2.00 from committed stops sharing this window (2 overlap) -> openness 0.33.
Routes the agent evaluated (3)
RTE-4100 · Central Houston · TUE · 0.1 miFEASIBLE · 0.82
  • ✓ service area: 0.1 mi from route center (limit 12.0 mi)
  • ✓ truck capacity: utilization 68% post-add (limit 90%), 290 cases headroom
RTE-4200 · West Houston / Energy Corridor · WED · 14.7 miINFEASIBLE
  • ✗ service area: 14.7 mi from route center (limit 12.0 mi)
  • ✓ truck capacity: utilization 52% post-add (limit 90%), 460 cases headroom
RTE-4400 · Southwest / Sugar Land · TUE · 18.7 miINFEASIBLE
  • ✗ service area: 18.7 mi from route center (limit 12.0 mi)
  • ✗ truck capacity: utilization 101% post-add (limit 90%), -10 cases headroom

Geographic clustering
0.67
Capacity buffer
0.39
Slot match (day + time)not scored · no preferred slot given
Slot availability (openness)
0.50

new prospect — no Sysco number yet

Woodlands Fresh Cafe

1201 Lake Woodlands Dr, The Woodlands, TX 77380
150 cases prefers THU 09:00–12:00 3 routes evaluated
✔ Recommended total score 77%

→ RTE-4300 · North Houston / The Woodlands · THU · 08:30–11:30 (score 0.77)

Geographic clustering
0.96
Capacity buffer
0.58
Slot match (day + time)
0.83
Slot availability (openness)
0.62
Why the agent chose thisAssign RTE-4300 - North Houston / The Woodlands · THU · 08:30-11:30.
  • Geographic fit: avg 0.6 mi to existing stops.
  • Capacity headroom: 150 cases of headroom left, putting the truck at about 81% full after this order (comfortably safe up to 75%).
  • Preferred-window match: covers 150 of the 180 preferred minutes on Thursday.
  • Slot openness: tier-weighted contention 0.60 from committed stops sharing this window (1 overlap) -> openness 0.62.
Trade-offEdges out the runner-up on overall route-slot score (0.77 vs 0.66); the runner-up is stronger on slot openness (0.91 vs 0.62) but weaker overall.
Runner-upRTE-4300 - North Houston / The Woodlands (THU) 12:30-15:30 — route-slot scored 0.66
Routes the agent evaluated (3)
RTE-4300 · North Houston / The Woodlands · THU · 0.3 miFEASIBLE · 0.77
  • ✓ service area: 0.3 mi from route center (limit 16.0 mi)
  • ✓ truck capacity: utilization 81% post-add (limit 90%), 150 cases headroom
RTE-4200 · West Houston / Energy Corridor · WED · 27.7 miINFEASIBLE
  • ✗ service area: 27.7 mi from route center (limit 12.0 mi)
  • ✓ truck capacity: utilization 58% post-add (limit 90%), 400 cases headroom
RTE-4100 · Central Houston · TUE · 28.4 miINFEASIBLE
  • ✗ service area: 28.4 mi from route center (limit 12.0 mi)
  • ✓ truck capacity: utilization 74% post-add (limit 90%), 230 cases headroom
About this data. The agent runs on mock Houston-area routes and geocoding so the workflow can be demonstrated end-to-end. Capacities, service radii, scoring weights, and thresholds are illustrative starting points — not validated Sysco policy. The route source and geocoder are designed to be swapped for real systems without changing the agent's logic.
Smart Assignment · an AI agent for delivery slot recommendation · built on Google's Agent Development Kit (ADK).
Source & docs on GitHub. Hard rules and scoring are deterministic and auditable; the recommend/escalate call, the route & slot pick, and the address correction are an LLM's — grounded in verified facts, constrained to feasible options, with a deterministic fallback. See the Architecture tab for every agent, sub-agent, and LLM call.
